explanation: The statement expresses that prop_0 can only occur if at least one of prop_1 or prop_2 currently holds (i.e., prop_0 requires authorization from at least one of these). This means: always, if prop_0 holds, then either prop_1 or prop_2 holds as well.

This specification means that transitioning to 'Start-up Mode' (which we can represent with an atomic proposition, say prop_0) is only permitted if prop_1 holds. In LTL, we usually represent 'A is only allowed if B' as 'whenever A occurs, B must be true at that time.' This is written as G(prop_0 -> prop_1).
Typically, 'prop_0 shall be allowed by prop_1' means that whenever prop_0 occurs, prop_1 must be true at the same time. In LTL, this is usually captured as: globally, if prop_0 then prop_1.

The sentence suggests that prop_0 is permissible in situations where either prop_1 or prop_2 are true. In LTL, this can be expressed by saying: whenever prop_0 holds, then prop_1 or prop_2 must also hold at that time.

The specification states that the transition to Start-up Mode can only occur if prop_0 is true (i.e., is allowed by prop_0). Since the only atomic proposition provided is prop_0, we'll express: Whenever the transition to Start-up Mode occurs, it is the case that prop_0 holds at that point. We will represent 'transition to Start-up Mode' as 'start_up_transition', even though it's not given in atomic propositions (likely to be replaced in a complete specification).

So, the general form is: G (start_up_transition -> prop_0)

If we are to write purely in terms of prop_0, then the only way to represent 'allowed by prop_0' is just 'prop_0'.

A typical interpretation of 'A shall be allowed by B' in LTL is that A can only occur if B occurs at the same time (or, alternatively, whenever A happens, B also happens). This can be encoded as: globally, if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 holds.

The specification says that transitioning to "Start-up Mode" must be allowed by prop_0 to prop_1; assuming this means that prop_1 (possibly indicating Start-up Mode) can only become true if prop_0 is true at the previous time step, i.e., prop_0 enables the transition to prop_1. Thus, whenever prop_1 holds but didn't hold previously, then prop_0 must have been true in the previous step.

This means that whenever prop_0 happens, prop_1 must have occurred (be true) at the same time or at some time before. In LTL, 'prop_0 is allowed by prop_1' is typically captured as: whenever prop_0 occurs, prop_1 must be true at that time or before, i.e., it's never the case that prop_0 occurs without prop_1. In LTL this can be expressed as: 'It is always the case that if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 holds now or has held before.' This is written as G(prop_0 -> prop_1).
If prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold at the same time (permission implies allowance concurrently). This is a safety property enforcing that whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 is also true.

A common interpretation of 'prop_0 shall be allowed by prop_1' is that prop_0 can only occur when prop_1 holds; that is, whenever prop_0 is true, then prop_1 must also be true at that moment. This is expressed by the safety property: globally, if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 is also true.
